title: Get Any Paid Windows Store Apps for FREE!
subtitle: A quick tutorial to get any Paid app from Windows Store for FREE !
body:
# How to Extract a Full Installer for Any Microsoft Store App (.appx/.appxbundle Guide)

Ever wanted a standalone installer file for an app from the Microsoft Store — something you could install offline, back up, or use on a system where the Store itself is being difficult? It's actually pretty simple once you know the right tools. This guide walks you through the entire process, from grabbing the raw package files to fixing the classic "installed but won't open" problem that trips up a lot of people.

## What You'll Need

Just three tools, all free:

1. **[Microsoft Store (web)](https://apps.microsoft.com/)** — to find your app and grab its page link
2. **[store.rg-adguard.net](https://store.rg-adguard.net/)** — to pull the direct package download links
3. **[WSAppBak](https://github.com/Wapitiii/WSAppBak/)** — to extract dependency resources if the app doesn't run after a plain install

No sketchy third-party installers, no ads, no shady downloaders — just direct links straight from Microsoft's own servers.

## Part 1: Getting the App Package

**Step 1.** Head to [apps.microsoft.com](https://apps.microsoft.com/) and search for the app you want.

**Step 2.** Open the app's page and copy the URL from your address bar.

**Step 3.** Go to [store.rg-adguard.net](https://store.rg-adguard.net/).

**Step 4.** Paste the link into the box and hit Enter.

**Step 5.** You'll get a list of download links. Only grab the files ending in `.appx` and `.appxbundle` — ignore everything else (like `.blockmap` or `.eappx` files).

> **Note:** Not every app link will return a usable download — some apps are region-locked, delisted, or packaged differently. If nothing shows up, don't worry, that's normal, and the fix below still applies once you do get files.

Once downloaded, just double-click the `.appxbundle` (or `.appx` if no bundle exists) to install it. For most apps, that's the whole process — done.

## Part 2: When the App Won't Open

Some apps install fine but refuse to launch, because they're missing additional resource packages that Windows would normally install automatically through the Store (language packs, scaling assets, etc.). Here's how to fix that.

**Step 6.** Go to the [WSAppBak GitHub repo](https://github.com/Wapitiii/WSAppBak/) and download the latest release ZIP.

**Step 7.** Unzip it and run `WSAppBak` — it will open a Command Prompt window.

**Step 8.** When prompted, you'll need to enter the path to the installed app's folder. To find it:

- **Step 9.** Open File Explorer, navigate to `Program Files`, enable "Show hidden items," and look for the **WindowsApps** folder.
- **Step 10.** If you get an access-denied error opening it, you'll need to take ownership of the folder for your user account first. (Search online for "take ownership of WindowsApps folder" if you're not familiar with this — it's a standard Windows permissions change, just be careful only to grant access, not delete anything.)
- **Step 11.** Inside `WindowsApps`, you'll see several folders related to your app. Pick the one with the **largest file size** — that's the actual installed app data, not just a stub.
- **Step 12.** Copy that folder's full path.

**Step 13.** Back in the WSAppBak CMD window, paste the path when it asks for **"Enter app path."** Hit Enter.

**Step 14.** It will then ask for an **output path**. Go back to the folder where you unzipped WSAppBak — it should have automatically created an `Output` folder there.

**Step 15.** Open that `Output` folder (it'll be empty at this point) and copy its path.

**Step 16.** Paste it into CMD where it asks for **"Enter the Output path."** Hit Enter.

**Step 17.** A popup will appear. Don't touch any of the listed options — just click **NONE**.

That's it — the extraction is done.

## Part 3: Reinstalling with All Dependencies

**Step 18.** Open the `Output` folder. You should now see several files: the main app installer plus its dependency packages.

**Step 19.** Uninstall the app you installed back in Part 1.

**Step 20.** Install everything from the `Output` folder — dependencies first, then the main app installer.

Your app should now launch and run exactly as it would through the Store — no more silent failures or crashes on open.

## Cleanup

Once everything's working, you can safely delete all the files you downloaded along the way (the `.appx`/`.appxbundle` files, the WSAppBak ZIP, everything). You won't need them again unless you're setting up another app down the line — just repeat the process from scratch when that time comes.

---

That's the full workflow — from Store link to a fully working offline installer. It looks like a lot of steps written out, but in practice it takes just a few minutes once you've done it once or twice.
bannerMsg: Caution: proceed carefully. The method has limitations; encrypted packages or packages containing other components cannot be downloaded through this method. Please support genuine software! This tutorial is released under the CC BY 4.0 license. (For the specific terms, please refer to this link: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)
